era_context: Early Bronze Age III. Fifth Dynasty Egypt under Djedkare Isesi (or possibly his successor, depending on chronology). Early Dynastic Period III in Mesopotamia. Indus Valley Civilization in mature phase. This year marks the end of a five-year span (2824–2820 BCE) characterized by relative stability in documented regions, though underlying political fragmentation is evident in Egypt.

events.title: Djedkare Isesi — Fifth Dynasty Egypt, reign continuation or succession
events.description: Djedkare Isesi's reign continues, or he may have been succeeded by his successor (possibly Unas or another Fifth Dynasty pharaoh, depending on chronology). The Fifth Dynasty is characterized by the consolidation of Old Kingdom administrative structures and the gradual decentralization of power. Inscriptions document the construction of temples, the organization of expeditions, and the maintenance of the state apparatus. The Pyramid Texts provide evidence of religious beliefs and funerary practices.

sources.name: Inscriptions from Fifth Dynasty tombs and temples

events.certainty_note: The Fifth Dynasty chronology is uncertain by ±50–100 years. Whether Djedkare Isesi was still reigning in 2820 BCE or had been succeeded depends on the chronological system used. This entry reflects the uncertainty.

events.title: Mesopotamian city-states — Early Dynastic Period III, continued competition
events.description: Sumerian city-states continue to compete for control of resources and trade routes. Cuneiform texts document military campaigns, the construction of defensive walls, and the organization of armies. The city of Uruk is rising in prominence, and Lugalzagesi (who would later unify much of Mesopotamia) may be beginning his rise to power, though the exact dating of his reign is uncertain. Conflicts over water rights and territorial boundaries remain frequent.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of fortifications and destruction layers

events.certainty_note: City-state competition during Early Dynastic III is well-attested. The dating of Lugalzagesi's reign is uncertain (c. 2340–2316 BCE or possibly earlier/later depending on chronology). Specific events in 2820 BCE are not documented.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization — mature phase, standardized production and trade
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its mature phase with thriving urban centers, standardized craft production, and extensive trade networks. Seals bearing the undeciphered Indus script are produced and used in trade. Evidence of maritime trade with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf is attested. The civilization's sophisticated urban planning, including drainage systems and public granaries, suggests a well-organized state or confederation of states.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Indus Valley sites
Indus seals, weights, and standardized artifacts

events.certainty_note: The existence and characteristics of the Indus Valley Civilization during this period are confirmed by archaeology.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of the Fifth Dynasty and Early Dynastic III remains debated among scholars, with potential variations of ±50–100 years. The dating of Lugalzagesi's reign is particularly uncertain; some scholars place it c. 2340–2316 BCE, while others propose earlier or later dates. The interpretation of Sumerian city-state conflicts as primarily resource-driven is contested by some scholars who emphasize ideological or dynastic factors.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2820 BCE is sparse and relies on archaeological evidence, inscriptions, and king lists. No contemporary narrative accounts survive. The record is heavily biased toward elite, monumental sources and toward regions with writing systems (Egypt, Mesopotamia, Indus Valley). Most of the world is undocumented for this year. The chronological uncertainty for this period is significant, with potential variations of ±50–100 years depending on the methodology used (king lists, radiocarbon dating, astronomical calculations).

graph_edges.note: Continuation or succession of Fifth Dynasty rule.
City-state competition of this period is part of a longer pattern that would culminate in Lugalzagesi's unification of Mesopotamia (dating uncertain).
